# declarative-hex-worlds

> Declarative, deterministic hex worlds for TypeScript games. A renderer-free core
> (worldgen + a koota ECS runtime that emits trait "signals") plus optional bindings
> (React + Three.js) that subscribe to those signals and draw. Assets are described by a
> Zod-validated `AssetSourceSpec` — tilesets (sprite-sheet atlases), GLTF models, and
> sprites — bound to the board with zero pack-specific code in the engine.

This file is the agent-facing guide: how to go from "I have assets" to "a rendered,
seamless hex board", using the exact public API. Everything below is real, current API
(the reference game `little-legends` renders its board this way).

## Mental model

1. **Assets → an `AssetSourceSpec`** (JSON). Roles: `tile` (png/glb hex), `tileset` (a png
   atlas + grid), `sprite` (png), `model` (glb/gltf). You author this three ways — hand,
   the `bind` CLI (agents), or the `init` CLI wizard (humans). All produce the SAME spec.
2. **A `GameboardPlan`** — where tiles/placements live on the hex grid. Build one with
   `createGameboardBuilder({ seed, shape }).build()`, or project your own world into one
   with `projectWorldToGameboardPlan(world, { geometry })`.
3. **Render** — the React bindings (`declarative-hex-worlds/react-elements`) take the plan
   + a source built from the spec and draw each placement. A tileset renders each atlas
   cell as an alpha-cutout quad, oversized to overlap into seamless continuous terrain.

The engine is AGNOSTIC: no KayKit (or any pack) values are hardcoded. Downloadable packs
(KayKit hex tiles / adventurers / skeletons) are offered as SUGGESTED DEFAULTS in the same
flow as scanning your own assets — never a special-cased path.

## Authoring the AssetSourceSpec

### Agent path (non-interactive) — `bind`

```bash
# Scan an assets dir → emit a Zod-validated AssetSourceSpec JSON to stdout (or --out).
# Convention: subdirs tiles/ tilesets/ sprites/ models/ set each file's role.
pnpm exec declarative-hex-worlds bind --dir public/assets --name my-pack --out my-pack.assets.json

# Tilesets need an atlas grid. Give the cols×rows a sheet tiles into; each tileset PNG is
# MEASURED (its pixel dimensions read from the IHDR chunk, no image decode) and the exact
# cell size derived. Supply BOTH --cols and --rows (or neither).
pnpm exec declarative-hex-worlds bind --dir public/assets --cols 5 --rows 10 --out my-pack.assets.json
```

`bind` is scriptable and silent — it prints the JSON and reports (to stderr) any files it
couldn't classify, tilesets still needing a grid, and tiles whose biome it couldn't guess.
Biome + gameplay-category guesses are heuristic DEFAULTS in the emitted spec.

### Human path (interactive) — `init`

```bash
# A blocking TTY wizard: walks you through confirming/overriding each tile biome,
# model/sprite gameplay category, and tileset grid, then writes the spec.
pnpm exec declarative-hex-worlds init --dir public/assets --name my-pack
```

`init` requires a TTY. In a non-interactive context (a pipe, CI, an agent) it refuses and
tells you to use `bind` — same flags, same spec, no prompts.

### Hand-authored

An `AssetSourceSpec` is plain JSON validated by `parseAssetSourceSpec` /
`safeParseAssetSourceSpec` (from `declarative-hex-worlds/asset-source`). Minimal shape:

```json
{
  "specVersion": 1,
  "name": "my-pack",
  "assetRoot": "/assets",
  "assets": [
    { "id": "grass", "role": "tile", "format": "png", "path": "tiles/grass.png", "biome": "grass" },
    { "id": "sheet", "role": "tileset", "format": "png", "path": "tilesets/sheet.png",
      "grid": { "cols": 5, "rows": 10, "cellWidth": 96, "cellHeight": 83 } },
    { "id": "knight", "role": "model", "format": "glb", "path": "models/knight.glb", "category": "pc" }
  ]
}
```

## Rendering a tileset board (the seamless-terrain flow)

This is the flow `little-legends` uses for a Civilization-style continuous board. Import
the React bindings from the `/react-elements` subpath (react + three are optional peers).

```tsx
import { createGameboardBuilder } from 'declarative-hex-worlds';
import { parseTilesetManifest, tilesetHexGeometry } from 'declarative-hex-worlds/asset-source';
import { GameboardObjects, HexWorld, Tile, Tileset } from 'declarative-hex-worlds/react-elements';
import manifestJson from './terrainTilesetManifest.json';

const manifest = parseTilesetManifest(manifestJson);
// The board is a FORESHORTENED tileset: rows pack tighter than a regular hex so full-cell
// quads interlock. Derive the geometry ONCE from the manifest and thread it everywhere
// (tiles, and any unit/city/camera math) so nothing floats off the compressed board.
const geometry = tilesetHexGeometry(manifest);

function Board({ plan, textureLoader }) {
  return (
    <HexWorld plan={plan} textureLoader={textureLoader} geometry={geometry}>
      <Tileset manifest={manifest} />
      {tiles.map((t) => (
        <Tile key={`${t.q},${t.r}`} at={`${t.q},${t.r}`} assetId={t.biome} biome={t.biome} />
      ))}
      <GameboardObjects />
    </HexWorld>
  );
}
```

Key points a renderer must respect:

- **One geometry, threaded.** `tilesetHexGeometry(manifest)` is the single source of the
  board's hex spacing. Pass it to `<HexWorld geometry>` AND use it in any sim↔world
  coordinate conversion (`axialToWorld(axial, y, geometry)` / `worldToAxial`). Two
  different geometries = units floating off the tiles.
- **Overlap is deliberate.** Each atlas cell is drawn as a quad slightly larger than the
  hex footprint (a built-in overlap factor) so neighbours interlock with no seams/gaps —
  this is how continuous terrain reads. Do NOT add a solid-mesh underlay behind it.
- **Alpha-cutout, not blend.** Tileset cells use a cutout material (`alphaTest`) so the
  transparent hex corners read as hard edges, not blended halos.
- **Biomes come from `<Tile biome>`**, not from the plan. The plan is the bare grid; the
  `<Tile>` children paint each cell's biome, resolved against the tileset manifest.

## Public subpaths (import map)

- `declarative-hex-worlds` — core: `createGameboardBuilder`, `createGameboardRuntime`,
  `projectWorldToGameboardPlan`, `axialToWorld`/`worldToAxial`, `combineSources`.
- `declarative-hex-worlds/asset-source` — the spec + sources: `parseAssetSourceSpec`,
  `createSourceFromSpec`, `createTilesetSource`, `tilesetHexGeometry`,
  `parseTilesetManifest`, `readPngDimensions`/`inferTilesetGrid`, `scanAssetFiles`,
  `buildAssetSourceSpec`, `BIOME_KEYWORDS`, `GAMEPLAY_CATEGORIES`.
- `declarative-hex-worlds/react` — runtime provider + hooks (`GameboardRuntimeProvider`,
  `useGameboardRuntime`).
- `declarative-hex-worlds/react-elements` — the R3F board: `HexWorld`, `Tile`, `Tileset`,
  `GameboardObjects`, camera helpers.
- `declarative-hex-worlds/three` — lower-level Three.js binding primitives.

## Determinism

Worldgen + the runtime are deterministic given a `seed`. Never use `Math.random()` in sim
code — the seed drives everything so a plan replays identically. The `bind`/`init` scan is
pure of randomness too.

## CLI reference

`pnpm exec declarative-hex-worlds --help` lists every command;
`declarative-hex-worlds <command> --help` gives per-flag reference. The asset-binding
commands are `bind` (agent), `init` (human), and `bootstrap` (clone the suggested CC0
KayKit packs into `public/assets/`).

## Full docs

https://jonbogaty.com/declarative-hex-worlds/ — guides, feature matrix, and the generated
API reference.
